title: Trauma and the Body's Agency: Reclaiming Choice After Trauma
excerpt: Explore how trauma impacts the body's agency and learn science-backed methods to reclaim choice and regulation after trauma.
body:
Introduction: Understanding Trauma Beyond the Mind

Many people who have experienced trauma find that traditional talk therapy alone isn’t enough to move them beyond feeling stuck. This is because trauma isn’t just a psychological event—it’s a physiological one. As Dr. Bessel van der Kolk eloquently states in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma reshapes both body and brain, often hijacking our nervous system and undermining our sense of agency—the fundamental feeling that we can make choices and exert control over our bodies and environment.

Reclaiming agency after trauma involves engaging with the body’s wisdom and its capacity to regulate itself. This article dives into the science of trauma’s impact on bodily agency and offers practical, research-grounded insights for self-directed healing.

What is Agency and Why Does Trauma Threaten It?

Agency, simply put, is the capacity to act intentionally and to make choices. It’s a core component of human autonomy and self-regulation. When we talk about bodily agency, we mean the ability to sense, respond to, and influence our own physical and emotional states.

Trauma disrupts this agency by overwhelming the nervous system. Dr. Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory explains how trauma can cause the autonomic nervous system to shift into defensive states—freeze, fight, or flight—where conscious choice narrows or disappears entirely. In these states, the body’s survival mechanisms override voluntary control, making it feel as if the body is acting without the mind’s consent.

Neuroscience of Trauma and Agency

Neuroscientific research shows that trauma impacts the brain regions responsible for executive function and emotion regulation, including the prefrontal cortex and limbic system. The amygdala becomes hyperactive, detecting threat even when none exists, while the prefrontal cortex—the seat of decision-making and inhibition—loses regulatory power.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic psychology, emphasizes that trauma fragments the integration between body and brain. This fragmentation leaves survivors feeling disconnected from their bodily sensations, further eroding agency.

How Trauma Lives in the Body

Trauma isn’t stored in memory alone; it’s encoded in muscle tension, posture, and autonomic nervous system responses. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, describes trauma as a "stuck" physiological reaction—an energy mobilized for fight or flight that never fully discharged.

This incomplete discharge manifests as chronic tension, pain, and autonomic dysregulation. You might notice unexplained body aches, shallow breathing, or a racing heart triggered by seemingly innocuous cues. These responses aren’t conscious choices but automatic survival strategies reinforced over time.

Somatic Markers of Lost Agency

  • Freeze and dissociation: Feeling numb or disconnected from your body.
  • Tension and constriction: Chronic muscle tightness or restricted movement.
  • Hyperarousal: Constant alertness or anxiety without clear triggers.
  • Hypoarousal: Fatigue, low energy, or emotional shutdown.

Recognizing these patterns is the first step toward reclaiming agency.

Reclaiming Choice: The Role of the Nervous System

Nervous system regulation is central to restoring agency. Deb Dana, a clinical social worker and expert on Polyvagal Theory, advocates for interventions that help survivors move out of defensive autonomic states and back into the "window of tolerance"—a zone of optimal arousal where we can think, feel, and act with intentionality.

Techniques that engage the vagus nerve can facilitate this shift. These include breathing exercises, gentle movement, and social engagement cues that signal safety to the nervous system. The goal is to rebuild the nervous system’s flexibility, allowing the body to respond adaptively instead of reflexively.

Somatic Practices to Empower Choice

  • Tracking Sensations: Mindful attention to bodily feelings without judgment helps increase interoceptive awareness, a foundation of agency.
  • Pendulation: Moving between states of activation and calm to gently expand tolerance for bodily sensations (Peter Levine).
  • Grounding Exercises: Using touch or pressure on the body to anchor attention and reinforce a sense of presence.
  • Movement and Posture: Intentional movement patterns that restore the natural flow of energy and counteract trauma-induced constriction.
"Trauma robs us of the ability to make choices about our own bodies. Reclaiming that choice is the heart of healing." — Bessel van der Kolk

Integrating Mind and Body: Internal Family Systems and Somatic Psychotherapy

Beyond nervous system regulation, integration of conflicting internal parts can restore agency. Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model identifies how trauma fragments the self into protective and vulnerable parts.

Somatic approaches complement IFS by adding bodily awareness to this internal dialogue. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy merges verbal and somatic methods to help survivors reconnect with their body while processing trauma narratives.

This integration fosters a compassionate relationship with oneself, allowing choice to emerge from a grounded, embodied self rather than reactive survival parts.

Why Self-Directed Healing Requires Patience and Compassion

Reclaiming bodily agency is rarely a linear process. As Gabor Maté explains, trauma is complex and deeply wired into our biology and relationships. Progress often involves setbacks, moments of overwhelm, and the need for repeated practice.

Self-directed healing benefits from a trauma-informed mindset: respect for your body’s signals, pacing your efforts, and seeking support when needed. Dan Siegel’s concept of "mindsight"—the ability to perceive internal experiences with curiosity—can guide you through this journey.

Conclusion: The Path Toward Embodied Freedom

Trauma may have hijacked your body’s agency, but it cannot permanently take it away. The body holds wisdom and capacity for regulation waiting to be reclaimed through informed, somatic practices.

By understanding trauma’s impact on the nervous system, developing interoceptive awareness, and integrating mind and body, you can rebuild your ability to make choices grounded in safety and presence. This is not about forcing control but about restoring natural rhythms and responses that support autonomy.

Remember: healing is an embodied process. Your body is not merely a victim of trauma but a powerful ally in your recovery.

References

  • Bessel van der Kolk, The Body Keeps the Score, 2014.
  • Peter Levine, Waking the Tiger, 1997.
  • Stephen Porges, Polyvagal Theory, 2011.
  • Deb Dana, The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy, 2018.
  • Pat Ogden, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, 2006.
  • Gabor Maté, In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts, 2008.
  • Dan Siegel, The Developing Mind, 1999.
  • Richard Schwartz, Internal Family Systems Therapy, 1995.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect the body’s sense of agency?
faqs.a: Trauma disrupts the nervous system, overwhelming the body’s natural regulation and narrowing conscious choice. This leads to automatic survival responses like freeze or fight, undermining bodily agency.

faqs.q: What are somatic practices for reclaiming agency after trauma?
faqs.a: Somatic practices include tracking bodily sensations mindfully, pendulation between activation and calm, grounding exercises, and intentional movement to restore natural energy flow.

faqs.q: Why is nervous system regulation important in trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Regulating the nervous system helps shift the body out of defensive states into a window of tolerance, enabling intentional action and reducing automatic, reactive behaviors.

faqs.q: Can self-directed healing work without a therapist?
faqs.a: Yes, with proper education and patience, self-directed somatic healing can be effective. However, complex trauma may require professional support to ensure safety and progress.
